questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman presents with a 3-day history of severe right upper quadrant pain, abdominal distension, and vomiting. She is on the combined oral contraceptive pill. On examination, she has tender hepatomegaly and shifting dullness. LFTs: AST 450 U/L, ALT 220 U/L, ALP 180 U/L, bilirubin 35 μmol/L. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The rapid onset of tender hepatomegaly, ascites, and AST>ALT in a young woman on OCP is classic for acute Budd-Chiari (hepatic vein thrombosis). Acute hepatitis B usually shows ALT>AST with prodrome; alcoholic hepatitis requires history and AST:ALT<2; cholecystitis has localized pain/Murphy's sign; pancreatitis has epigastric pain and raised amylase.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman presents with a 2-month history of gradual abdominal swelling and leg oedema. She has no history of alcohol use or jaundice. Examination reveals ascites, splenomegaly, and dilated abdominal wall veins that flow away from the umbilicus. LFTs: bilirubin 32 μmol/L, ALT 68 U/L, AST 92 U/L, ALP 210 U/L, albumin 32 g/L. Ultrasound shows caudate lobe hypertrophy and absent hepatic venous flow. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Caudate lobe hypertrophy and absent hepatic vein flow on ultrasound are diagnostic of Budd-Chiari. Dilated abdominal veins with upward flow suggest IVC/portal hypertension from hepatic vein obstruction. Cirrhosis shows shrunken nodular liver; PBC has high ALP/GTT and positive AMA; hepatitis C has serology; non-cirrhotic portal hypertension has patent hepatic veins.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old man from Nepal presents with progressive bilateral leg swelling and prominent abdominal wall veins for 6 months. He has no history of liver disease or jaundice. Examination reveals dilated veins over the abdomen with upward flow, and mild ascites. LFTs are normal except for ALP 150 U/L. A CT venogram shows a thin membrane in the inferior vena cava just above the hepatic veins. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Budd-Chiari syndrome due to IVC web
questions.explanation: Membranous obstruction of the IVC (IVC web) is a cause of Budd-Chiari, more common in Asia, presenting with lower limb edema, abdominal collaterals, and ascites. Constrictive pericarditis has Kussmaul's sign and pericardial calcification; right heart failure has elevated JVP; cirrhosis has nodular liver; nephrotic syndrome has proteinuria.

questions.stem: A 25-year-old woman on the oral contraceptive pill presents with acute onset of severe epigastric pain, nausea, vomiting, and confusion of 12 hours duration. On examination, she is confused and has asterixis. LFTs: ALT 2000 U/L, AST 4000 U/L, bilirubin 100 μmol/L, INR 3.0. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: In acute Budd-Chiari, AST may be significantly higher than ALT (ratio >2) due to ischemic injury, and it can present as fulminant hepatic failure. Fulminant hepatitis B has high ALT>AST and serology; alcoholic hepatitis requires history and AST:ALT<2; Wilson disease has low ceruloplasmin and Kayser-Fleischer rings; paracetamol overdose has very high ALT >10,000.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with known Budd-Chiari syndrome treated with a transjugular intrahepatic portosystemic shunt (TIPS) 3 years ago presents with dull right upper quadrant pain and weight loss. Serum AFP is 500 ng/mL. CT scan shows a 4 cm enhancing lesion in the right lobe with arterial hyperenhancement and venous washout. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Long-standing Budd-Chiari increases HCC risk due to chronic ischemia and regeneration. CT features of arterial hyperenhancement and venous washout are classic for HCC. Cholangiocarcinoma shows delayed enhancement; metastases have different patterns; adenoma occurs in young women on OCP; FNH has central scar.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman presents with acute onset of abdominal pain, abdominal distension, and hepatomegaly. Initial ultrasound shows ascites but no flow in the hepatic veins. What is the most appropriate next diagnostic test to confirm Budd-Chiari syndrome?
options: Doppler ultrasound of the hepatic veins
questions.explanation: MR venography is the confirmatory test of choice when Doppler ultrasound is equivocal or suggests Budd-Chiari, as it provides high-resolution imaging of the hepatic veins and inferior vena cava without radiation or invasiveness.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with polycythaemia vera presents with slowly progressive abdominal distension and lower limb oedema. He has stigmata of chronic liver disease. A Doppler ultrasound shows an absent hepatic vein waveform and a large regenerative nodule. Which finding would confirm the diagnosis of Budd-Chiari syndrome?
options: Absence of flow in all three hepatic veins on Doppler ultrasound
Demonstration of a membrane in the inferior vena cava on MR venography
Presence of intrahepatic collaterals on CT
Histological evidence of hepatic veno-occlusive disease
Elevated hepatic venous pressure gradient >10 mmHg
questions.explanation: Doppler ultrasound is highly specific for Budd-Chiari syndrome when it shows absence of flow in the hepatic veins, especially when combined with clinical context. This is a key diagnostic feature.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman on the combined oral contraceptive pill presents with sudden severe right upper quadrant pain and hepatomegaly. She has no history of liver disease. An initial abdominal ultrasound shows moderate ascites but the hepatic veins are not clearly visualised. What is the single most appropriate investigation to confirm the diagnosis?
questions.explanation: MR venography is the recommended non-invasive confirmatory test for Budd-Chiari syndrome when Doppler ultrasound is inconclusive. It reliably demonstrates thrombus within the hepatic veins and IVC.

questions.stem: A 42-year-old man with a history of myeloproliferative disease presents with jaundice, tender hepatomegaly, and ascites. A Doppler ultrasound reveals a patent portal vein but no detectable flow in the right and middle hepatic veins. Which interpretation is most accurate for diagnosing acute Budd-Chiari syndrome?
options: The findings are consistent with a normal variant
Findings are diagnostic of acute Budd-Chiari syndrome
CT venography is needed to confirm absence of flow
Liver biopsy is required to exclude veno-occlusive disease
Hepatic venography is required to confirm the diagnosis
questions.explanation: Doppler ultrasound showing absent flow in two hepatic veins in a patient with clinical features is sufficient for diagnosis of acute Budd-Chiari syndrome. No further imaging is immediately required.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman with essential thrombocythaemia develops progressive ascites and abdominal pain. She is suspected to have Budd-Chiari syndrome. Which single investigation is most sensitive for detecting hepatic vein thrombosis in this setting?
questions.explanation: MR venography has the highest sensitivity for detecting hepatic vein thrombosis, especially in chronic or subacute cases where flow may be slow. It is the gold standard non-invasive test for Budd-Chiari syndrome.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman presents with a 3-day history of severe right upper quadrant pain, hepatomegaly, and ascites. Doppler ultrasound confirms acute Budd-Chiari syndrome with thrombosis of all three hepatic veins. She has no prior medical history. What is the most appropriate first-line therapy?
options: Low molecular weight heparin followed by warfarin
questions.explanation: Anticoagulation with low molecular weight heparin bridging to warfarin is first-line for acute Budd-Chiari syndrome to prevent thrombus propagation and promote recanalisation. TIPS is reserved for failure of anticoagulation or severe decompensation; thrombolysis is not first-line.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with known Budd-Chiari syndrome secondary to a myeloproliferative neoplasm has been on warfarin (target INR 2-3) for 12 months. He now presents with worsening ascites and right upper quadrant pain. Repeat imaging shows persistent thrombosis of the hepatic veins despite therapeutic INR. What is the most appropriate definitive therapy?
options: Switch to low molecular weight heparin
Increase warfarin dose to target INR 3-4
questions.explanation: In patients who progress despite adequate anticoagulation, TIPS is the next step to decompress the liver. Liver transplant is reserved for those not suitable for TIPS or with end-stage disease.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old woman presents with acute onset of jaundice, encephalopathy, and coagulopathy. Imaging shows acute thrombosis of all three hepatic veins and a small, shrunken liver. She has no prior liver disease. What is the most appropriate first-line definitive therapy?
questions.explanation: In acute Budd-Chiari syndrome presenting with fulminant hepatic failure, TIPS is the first-line intervention to rapidly decompress the liver and improve hepatic function. Liver transplant is considered if TIPS fails or is not anatomically feasible.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old man with known polycythaemia vera (on hydroxycarbamide) is diagnosed with Budd-Chiari syndrome. Imaging shows non-occlusive thrombus in the left hepatic vein with mild ascites. He is haemodynamically stable with normal bilirubin. What is the most appropriate definitive therapy?
options: Start low molecular weight heparin then warfarin in addition to hydroxycarbamide
questions.explanation: Anticoagulation is the mainstay of Budd-Chiari syndrome treatment regardless of underlying cause. Cytoreduction with hydroxycarbamide should be continued, and anticoagulation added. Aspirin alone is insufficient.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old pregnant woman (32 weeks gestation) presents with acute Budd-Chiari syndrome. She has no other medical history. Which of the following therapies is contraindicated?
questions.explanation: Warfarin is teratogenic and contraindicated in pregnancy. Low molecular weight heparin is safe and the preferred anticoagulant. Other interventions may be considered if necessary.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with a 3-day history of right upper quadrant pain, nausea, and abdominal distension. She is not on any medication. On examination, she has tender hepatomegaly and shifting dullness. Blood tests show mildly elevated ALT and AST, bilirubin 25 μmol/L, INR 1.1. Abdominal ultrasound reveals absence of flow in the right and middle hepatic veins. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Refer for transjugular intrahepatic portosystemic shunt (TIPS)
Arrange contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen
questions.explanation: In acute Budd-Chiari syndrome without contraindications, immediate anticoagulation is the first step to prevent extension of thrombus. CT is not urgent; paracentesis may be needed but not before anticoagulation; ursodeoxycholic acid has no role in acute management; TIPS is not first-line.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old man with known essential thrombocythaemia on aspirin and hydroxycarbamide presents with mild epigastric discomfort. Examination reveals mild hepatomegaly but no ascites. Liver function tests are normal except for gamma-GT 80 U/L. Doppler ultrasound shows subacute thrombus in the left hepatic vein with cavernous transformation. INR is 1.0. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Start low molecular weight heparin (e.g., enoxaparin)
questions.explanation: Anticoagulation with LMWH is first-line therapy; bridging to warfarin is typical. Bone marrow biopsy is not urgent; liver biopsy is not indicated; antiplatelet agents alone are insufficient for venous thrombosis.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman with no known medical history presents with acute onset of severe abdominal pain, vomiting, and confusion. On examination, she is hypotensive (BP 80/50 mmHg), tachycardic, and has tense ascites. LFTs show ALT 800 U/L, AST 600 U/L, bilirubin 100 μmol/L, INR 2.5. Urgent bedside ultrasound suggests hepatic vein thrombosis. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Give vitamin K and fresh frozen plasma
questions.explanation: This patient has fulminant hepatic failure with hypotension; initial priority is fluid resuscitation. Paracentesis risks haemorrhage; anticoagulation is dangerous given coagulopathy; vitamin K/FFP are insufficient alone; transplantation is not immediate.

questions.stem: A 38-year-old man with known Budd-Chiari syndrome on long-term warfarin for recurrent thrombosis presents with haematemesis. He is haemodynamically stable, pulse 95 bpm, BP 110/70 mmHg. Endoscopy shows oesophageal varices with a visible clot. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Stop warfarin and give vitamin K
questions.explanation: For variceal bleeding, immediate pharmacological therapy with terlipressin is recommended before endoscopy. Stopping warfarin is important but not more urgent; TIPS and band ligation are later steps; FFP is not first-line.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old pregnant woman at 26 weeks gestation with a history of Budd-Chiari syndrome diagnosed 2 years ago presents to the maternity assessment unit with mild right upper quadrant pain and worsening peripheral oedema. She is on prophylactic enoxaparin 40 mg daily. On examination, she has hepatomegaly and mild ascites. Doppler ultrasound shows a new thrombus in the left hepatic vein. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Arrange CT venography of the abdomen
questions.explanation: In pregnancy with acute thrombosis, immediate action is to ensure therapeutic anticoagulation. LMWH at therapeutic dose is safe and preferred over IV heparin. CT radiation should be avoided; caesarean is not indicated; TIPS is not first-line.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman presents with acute right upper quadrant pain, hepatomegaly, and ascites. She has no prior medical history. Vital signs are normal. Which clinical or diagnostic finding is most suggestive of an urgent need for intervention?
options: Elevated serum ALT (5x upper limit of normal)
Hepatic vein thrombus on Doppler ultrasound
questions.explanation: In acute Budd-Chiari, the presence of hepatic vein thrombus on imaging is diagnostic and mandates urgent anticoagulation to prevent progression to liver failure. While hepatomegaly and ascites are typical, they are not specific; the finding of thrombus confirms the diagnosis and triggers immediate treatment.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with known chronic Budd-Chiari syndrome maintained on warfarin presents with a two-day history of abdominal distension and mild confusion. He has no fever. Which clinical finding most strongly indicates decompensation requiring escalation?
options: Increasing abdominal girth (2 cm over baseline)
questions.explanation: The development of hepatic encephalopathy in a patient with chronic Budd-Chiari signals worsening liver function and potential acute-on-chronic liver failure. This requires urgent assessment and consideration of TIPS or transplant. Ascites and jaundice are common but encephalopathy is a more specific marker of hepatic decompensation.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman with no known liver disease presents with sudden severe epigastric pain, vomiting, and hypotension. She is found to have hepatomegaly and tense ascites. What life-threatening diagnosis must be excluded as the cause of her presentation?
options: Fulminant hepatic failure due to acute Budd-Chiari
questions.explanation: Acute Budd-Chiari can present with rapid onset of liver failure, including coagulopathy, encephalopathy, and shock. This is a medical emergency requiring immediate transfer to a liver unit. The other options are possible but less likely given the hepatomegaly and ascites; fulminant hepatic failure is the key red flag.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old man with chronic Budd-Chiari syndrome and refractory ascites presents with fever and abdominal tenderness. Paracentesis is performed. Which laboratory finding would indicate a life-threatening complication?
options: Ascitic fluid cytology positive for malignant cells
questions.explanation: Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis (SBP) is a common complication in patients with cirrhotic ascites, including those with Budd-Chiari. A neutrophil count >250 cells/μL is diagnostic and requires urgent antibiotics. The other options are not thresholds for emergency intervention.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman with a history of Budd-Chiari syndrome in remission presents three weeks postpartum with acute right upper quadrant pain. She is on therapeutic enoxaparin. Which diagnostic finding is most suggestive of recurrent thrombosis?
options: Absence of hepatic vein flow on Doppler ultrasound
Portal vein diameter of 13 mm on ultrasound
questions.explanation: In a patient with prior Budd-Chiari, recurrent thrombosis is a key concern, especially postpartum. Doppler ultrasound demonstrating absent or reversed flow in the hepatic veins confirms recurrence. D-dimer is nonspecific in postpartum, and hepatomegaly is common; the imaging finding is diagnostic.
